title-group.article-title: Artificial Intelligence in Vitreoretinal Surgery: A Systematic Review of Current Applications and Future Directions

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/02tdmfk69grid.412915.a0000 0000 9565 2378Department of Ophthalmology, Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, Belfast, Northern Ireland UK

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, adaptation, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if changes were made. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article's Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article's Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: To examine the current landscape of artificial intelligence (AI) applications in vitreoretinal (VR) diseases and surgery, with the aim of identifying knowledge gaps and guiding future directions in this rapidly evolving field.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Systematic review including original studies involving the use of AI and focusing on VR pathologies. A comprehensive electronic search of the literature was carried out in multiple databases.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Thirty-seven studies were included. Most evaluated machine learning or deep learning models for preoperative prognostication using optical coherence tomography with or without clinical variables. Predictive performance for postoperative best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) was high in several cohorts (R2 up to 0.80; area under the receiver operating characteristic curve [AUROC] > 0.95), with models consistently highlighting outer retinal biomarkers as key determinants of visual recovery after epiretinal membrane and macular hole surgery. For anatomical outcomes, deep learning models frequently achieved > 90% accuracy in predicting macular hole closure and retinal reattachment/reattachment-related endpoints. Intraoperative computer-vision systems demonstrated feasibility for real-time instrument detection and tracking, reporting precision above 90% in experimental or early clinical settings. Large language models showed moderate-to-high agreement with expert surgical planning (80–93%) and potential utility in education and workflow support; however, across domains, most studies were retrospective and single-center, with limited external validation.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: AI may transform vitreoretinal surgery, from outcome prediction to intraoperative guidance and workflow support. Despite strong performance in research settings, broader clinical adoption requires prospective validation to ensure reliability, transparency, and real-world benefit.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s40123-026-01347-8.

abstract.title: Plain Language Summary
abstract.p: This study looked at how artificial intelligence (AI) is being used in diseases and surgeries of the back part of the eye, known as the vitreoretinal (VR) area. We reviewed studies that used AI to help predict surgical outcomes, guide surgery, or improve efficiency in eye care, and found that AI models could accurately predict how well patients would see after surgery and whether the retina would heal properly. Some systems could even track surgical tools in real time, helping surgeons during operations. AI chatbots also showed promise in supporting doctors with planning and education. However, most studies were based on past data and small samples, meaning these tools are not yet ready for everyday use. Future research needs to test AI in real-world settings to make sure it is accurate, safe, and truly helpful for both patients and eye surgeons.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s40123-026-01347-8.

custom-meta.meta-value: © Springer Healthcare Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

tr.td: •This review examined how artificial intelligence (AI) is currently applied in vitreoretinal (VR) surgery and identified opportunities, limitations, and future directions for its clinical integration.
•AI offers new ways to improve surgical planning, risk assessment, and workflow efficiency in VR care, but its clinical value is still uncertain.
•AI is expanding beyond prediction into intraoperative guidance, risk stratification, and workflow automation, showing potential to support rather than replace surgical judgment.
•Broader clinical use will require transparent, multicenter validation and diverse datasets to ensure fairness, interpretability, and real-world reliability.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Artificial intelligence (AI) and high-resolution imaging technologies have opened up substantial opportunities for innovation. AI itself has a number of subtypes, one of the most important of which is machine learning (ML). ML employs algorithms for learning data patterns and improving performance over a period with additional data. Within ML, there is the even more advanced field of deep learning (DL), where multilayer artificial neural networks are used to automatically learn features from complex datasets without explicit programming. DL is especially good at tasks based on images, such as classification, segmentation, and prediction; [1] this makes it very useful in fields like ophthalmology, where imaging techniques like optical coherence tomography (OCT) and fundus photography create a large volume of high-resolution data. Such data are invaluable for training AI models. In retinal imaging, DL models have been shown to be highly effective in the identification of conditions such as diabetic retinopathy and age-related macular degeneration [2, 3]. Fundus photographs and OCT scans, aside from assisting in diagnosis, can also offer details relating to systemic health, including cardiovascular and neurodegenerative risk [4, 5]. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs), generative adversarial networks (GANs) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are specialized DL models designed to recognize complex patterns in data. CNNs excel in image analysis, GANs in data generation, and RNNs in processing sequential information. These models have been particularly successful in interpreting large visual datasets [6, 7].

However, despite these advances, most AI applications in ophthalmology have focused on medical retina conditions, with fewer considerations given to surgical conditions such as macular hole (MH), epiretinal membrane (ERM), and rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RRD). Although less prevalent than other retinal diseases, they are complex and require timely intervention. There is significant potential for AI to improve care by assisting with diagnosis, supporting non-vitreoretinal (VR) specialists in avoiding delayed referrals, enabling more accurate prediction of surgical outcomes, and refining patient selection criteria [8–10].

This review focuses on artificial intelligence applications in vitreoretinal surgery and in surgery-relevant vitreoretinal pathologies when outputs directly inform surgical planning, prognostication, intraoperative support, or postoperative outcome assessment. The review systematically examines the current landscape of AI applications in VR diseases and surgery. It critically evaluates the methodological quality, clinical relevance, and translational potential of existing research, with the aim of identifying knowledge gaps and guiding future directions in this rapidly evolving field (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
Schematic overview of the landscape of artificial intelligence (AI) applications in vitreoretinal surgery (VR). BCVA best-corrected visual acuity
This article is based on previously conducted studies and does not contain any new studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This systematic review was conducted following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines. The article is based on previously conducted studies and does not contain any new studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

We included studies of any design focusing on VR surgery and pathologies, including randomized controlled trials, non-randomized trials, observational studies, and case series. Selection criteria included original peer-reviewed studies, involving the use of AI and centered around VR pathologies. Robotic-assisted surgery was excluded from this review because it represents a broad field that would require a separate, dedicated analysis.
The search included only original articles. We chose to focus only on articles in English for practical purposes. This review has been registered in PROSPERO (CRD420251074415, ID: 1074415).

A comprehensive electronic search of the literature was carried out in the following databases from January 2015 to September 2025: PubMed/MEDLINE, Scopus, Embase, Web of Science, and the Cochrane Library. Additionally, ClinicalTrials.gov was searched for ongoing or unpublished trials. Search terms included the following: artificial intelligence, machine learning, large language models, natural processing language, neural networks, computer vision, algorithms, AI, ML, DL, image processing, computer-assisted surgical procedures, operative methods, intraoperative care, surgical planning, outcome prediction, vitrectomy, retinal surgery, epiretinal membrane surgery, macular hole surgery, and retinal detachment repair. A total of 740 articles were identified in the databases. Duplicate articles were removed, and the remaining articles were screened, quality assessed, and data extracted independently by two reviewers (EB and RA) using standard methods. A final yield of 37 studies were included. The selection process is detailed in the PRISMA flow diagram (Fig. 2).Fig. 2
PRISMA 2020 flow diagram for new systematic reviews which included searches of databases and registers only

Because of the methodological heterogeneity among studies, risk of bias was assessed using a design-adapted framework. Instead of applying a single checklist across non-comparable designs, we used tool-aligned criteria by study type (PROBAST [Prediction Model Risk of Bias Assessment Tool]-aligned principles for prediction models, QUADAS [Quality Assessment of Diagnostic Accuracy Studies]-2-aligned principles for diagnostic accuracy studies, and JBI [Joanna Briggs Institute]-aligned principles for cross-sectional analyses).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A table containing detailed information on all the included articles can be found in the supplementary material.

sec.title: Postoperative Visual Acuity Prediction
sec.p: Numerous studies have focused on the use of AI to predict postoperative best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) in patients undergoing VR surgery, particularly for conditions such as idiopathic ERM and MH. These efforts typically utilized preoperative OCT imaging, often combined with clinical parameters (metadata), to train models aimed at predicting BCVA outcomes.

Desideri et al. used an AI-based platform to identify OCT biomarkers associated with visual outcomes following macula-off RRD surgery. They found that increased outer nuclear layer (ONL) and photoreceptor-retinal pigment epithelium (PR-RPE) thicknesses at 4–6 weeks postoperatively correlated with improved visual acuity at 12 months, while hyperreflective foci (HRF) and intraretinal fluid (IRF) were linked to poorer outcomes. A random forest model confirmed first postoperative baseline BCVA as the strongest predictor of BCVA at 12 months, followed by baseline ONL thickness and HF presence. The study showed the importance of early postoperative OCT biomarkers, including ONL thickness and HF presence, for understanding the structural factors that predict visual recovery following RRD repair [11].

Similarly, Desideri et al. applied the same AI-based platform to quantify preoperative OCT biomarkers in patients undergoing vitrectomy for idiopathic ERM. The authors found that thinner preoperative ONL and PR-RPE complex thicknesses were associated with poorer visual acuity at 12 months postoperative, while thicker inner retinal layers such as retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) and ganglion cell layer–inner plexiform layer (GCL+IPL) also correlated with limited functional recovery. In contrast, greater IRF volume and the presence of HF were negatively related to postoperative improvement. A random forest classifier (ML) achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.71 in predicting visual gain, identifying preoperative PR-RPE, RNFL, and ONL thickness as the most influential features. These findings highlighted the role of both inner and outer retinal alterations as predictors of visual outcome after ERM surgery and support the use of AI-derived automated OCT biomarker analysis for patient counseling and surgical decision-making [12].

Moving from ML algorithms to more complex DL ones, Wen et al. developed a DL model using preoperative OCT images and clinical data to predict 6-month postoperative BCVA in idiopathic ERM patients [6]. Their multimodal deep fusion network (MDFN) achieved a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.070 logMAR and root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.11 logMAR on a test dataset, with an R2 of 0.80, significantly outperforming conventional regression models (R2 = 0.49). The high percentage of predictions within ±0.20 logMAR (94.32%) suggests substantial clinical utility in surgical planning and patient counseling.

Similarly, Kwon et al. introduced a generative DL model (GDLM) based on a variational autoencoder to synthesize postoperative OCT images following full-thickness macular hole (FTMH) surgery [7]. A variational autoencoder is a type of DL model that learns the essential features of a dataset and then uses them to generate new, realistic examples, in this case creating OCT images that resemble those seen after surgery. These models are used to simulate postoperative outcomes, improve surgical planning, and expand datasets for training and validating AI algorithms. Their model was trained on 120,000 pre- and postoperative image pairs and evaluated on foveolar height, mean foveal thickness, and restoration of external limiting membrane (ELM) and ellipsoid zone (EZ). The predicted OCT images showed high concordance with ground truth, achieving accuracy of 94.7% and F1 score of 0.891 for neurosensory retinal area segmentation. ELM and EZ restoration were predicted with 88% and 92% accuracy, respectively, suggesting that synthetic OCT generation could play a critical role in preoperative planning and visual prognosis estimation.

Kim et al. developed a DL model to predict central foveal thickness (CFT) and visual outcomes following non-complicated idiopathic ERM surgery. Using preoperative OCT images and clinical data from 688 eyes, a VGG16-based model was trained to estimate final CFT and CFT changes 1 year after surgery. VGG16 is a deep convolutional neural network originally designed for image classification tasks, and it is particularly effective at recognizing visual patterns and learning features from images at different levels of detail. The model achieved the highest accuracy when incorporating both clinical and surgical information, with good performance for CFT prediction. However, the model was unable to reliably predict final BCVA or BCVA changes [8].

A similar methodology was used to predict BCVA with a DL model. Three popular deep convolutional neural network architectures, Inception-v3, VGG-19, and ResNet-101, were tested on a dataset of 848 eyes. These architectures are widely used in image analysis and have been successfully adapted for medical image classification, segmentation, and prediction tasks. Among them, ResNet-101 demonstrated the best performance (area under the receiver operating characteristic curve [AUROC] 0.97, accuracy 90%, F1 score 0.90). Analysis of a grad-CAM [Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping] heatmap, a visual explanation technique that highlights the regions of an image that the model relies on when making predictions, showed that the model’s decision-making aligned closely with clinical reasoning. The DL model outperformed general ophthalmologists and matched or slightly exceeded retina specialists in predicting significant postoperative visual improvement, and this may play a role in idiopathic ERM surgical prognostication. Importantly, the authors improved the clinical applicability of their DL approach by validating the model using an external dataset from a different hospital, demonstrating generalizability and robustness in real-world clinical settings [13].

Lachance et al. investigated DL models for predicting BCVA improvement following MH surgery. Using preoperative high-definition OCT (HD-OCT) B-scans, and metadata from 121 eyes, they developed separate and combined models to predict an improvement of ≥ 15 ETDRS letter at 6 months. The clinical model achieved an AUROC of 80.6, while the OCT-based CNN achieved 72.8. A hybrid model combining both achieved an AUROC of 81.9, with baseline BCVA unsurprisingly contributing most to prediction performance. In clinical AI models, a high AUROC means the model can reliably distinguish between patients with and without a condition across different thresholds. The study highlights that while DL models using OCT data are promising, combining them with clinical information offers only marginal improvement in predicting postoperative VA outcomes [14].

In another study, the authors developed a DL model, called Heterogeneous Data Fusion Net, to predict visual outcomes after ERM surgery using preoperative OCT images and clinical factors from 529 patients. The model is designed to integrate different types of data, combining imaging inputs with clinical variables so the network can learn complex relationships across sources. This approach allows the model to capture more predictive information than image-only models. It achieved 88.7% accuracy, with an AUC of 97.8%, 87.0% sensitivity, and 92.9% specificity for predicting a ≥ 2-line Snellen improvement at 12 months. Heatmaps highlighted the EZ and superficial retina as key predictive regions, supporting the model’s potential for personalized surgical planning in ERM cases [15].

Similarly, Kucukgoz et al. developed an uncertainty-aware regression model (U-ARM) to predict postoperative BCVA following MH surgery using preoperative spectral-domain OCT images. This type of model not only generates a prediction but also estimates how confident the algorithm is in its output, which is important in clinical decision-making. U-ARM helps clinicians understand when predictions are reliable and when additional caution or data may be needed. The model achieved an MAE of 6.07, RMSE of 9.11, and R2 of 0.47 in internal tests, and an MAE of 6.49, RMSE of 9.49, and R2 of 0.42 in external tests [16]. The model showed consistent performance across internal and external tests, with reasonably accurate prediction of postoperative BCVA. The R2 values of 0.47 (internal) and 0.42 (external) suggest that the model explains a substantial proportion of the variability in outcomes, which is important considering the complex and multifactorial nature of visual recovery after VR surgery. The authors performed external validation using independent OCT datasets acquired from different sources, which showed reliable performance beyond the training distribution.

Another ML model, the Light Gradient-Boosting Machine (LightGBM), using preoperative clinical data and OCT features, was employed to predict postoperative BCVA in patients undergoing vitrectomy for ERM. LightGBM is an efficient gradient boosting framework that builds multiple decision trees in sequence, each correcting the errors of the previous one, which makes it particularly powerful for handling structured clinical and imaging data. The model achieved RMSE values of 0.14, 0.12, and 0.13 logMAR at 1, 3, and 6 months, respectively. Unsurprisingly, the incorporation of early postoperative BCVA improved prediction accuracy (RMSE 0.10 logMAR at 3 and 6 months). SHAP (Shapley additive explanations) analysis, which explains how models weigh different features in their predictions, identified preoperative BCVA and the ectopic inner foveal layer (EIFL) area as key predictors. The study showed that postoperative VA in ERM patients can be reliably estimated using OCT and clinical parameters, and visualizing predictor contributions may improve clinical decision-making [17].

A DL classifier (DLC) was used to predict BCVA improvement 1 year after vitrectomy for stage II ERM using preoperative OCT B-scan images. The DLC is designed to sort images into categories, in this case distinguishing patients likely to gain significant vision from those unlikely to do so by automatically learning key image features. In a cohort of 411 patients, the model achieved sensitivity of 87.3%, specificity of 86.2%, a positive predictive value (PPV) of 84.0%, and a negative predictive value (NPV) of 89.1% in distinguishing patients with significant VA improvement (≥ 15 ETDRS letters) from those without. Secondary analysis identified fibrillary changes, particularly when enhanced through image processing, as strong predictors of visual outcomes (OR 5.42). Additionally, the model was evaluated on an independent external dataset acquired at a separate center using multiple OCT devices [18].

Obata et al. analyzed a dataset of 259 eyes and developed a DL model using preoperative OCT images to predict postoperative VA after MH surgery. The model achieved 46% precision, with a correlation coefficient of r = 0.62 (p < 0.0001), while a multivariate regression model based on preoperative VA, MH size, and age performed slightly worse (40% precision, r = 0.55) [19].

Guo et al. developed a multimodal AI model combining preoperative ultra-wide-field (UWF) fundus images, macular OCT, and clinical data from 184 patients who underwent RRD surgery. The model achieved strong performance (AUC 0.91; accuracy 86%, sensitivity 94%, specificity 80%) in predicting vision impairment (BCVA < 6/18) at 3 months, with heatmap analysis showing the macular region as most predictive [20].

Finally, Godani et al. evaluated supervised ML models to predict visual outcomes after MH surgery using preoperative OCT data from 158 eyes (1665 scans). The random forest regression model showed the best performance, with a mean squared error of 0.038 on internal validation and 0.042 on external testing. The model predicted postoperative visual acuity within 0.1 logMAR in 61% of scans, within 0.2 logMAR in 78%, and within 0.3 logMAR in 87%. A random forest is an ML method that combines the predictions of many decision trees; each tree examines different parts of the data, and the results are aggregated (by averaging for regression tasks) to produce more accurate and reliable predictions than any single tree alone. Postoperative MH closure status and MH area index emerged as the strongest predictors of visual improvement, highlighting the potential of ML to guide surgical planning and patient counseling for MH surgery [21].

sec.title: Anatomical Outcome Predictions
sec.p: Predicting anatomical outcomes following VR surgery has been a prominent area of focus for AI applications, especially in the management of MH, ERM, and RRDs. These outcomes, including MH closure, restoration of specific retinal layers, and reattachment success, are critical markers for the evaluation of surgical efficacy and long-term morphofunctional prognosis.

Kim et al. explored this topic using a Pix2Pix GAN model to predict structural changes in the retina following ERM surgery [22]. A Pix2Pix GAN is a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) used for image-to-image translation, where a generator learns to transform an input image, like a sketch, into a realistic output image such as an OCT or fundus picture. It is trained adversarially with a discriminator that checks whether the generated image looks real and correctly corresponds to the input. Their model, trained on 500 pre- and postoperative OCT image pairs, generated synthetic postoperative scans with a mean structural similarity index measure (SSIM) of 0.913, suggesting that the synthetic images closely approximated the real ones. The use of GANs in this context not only enables prediction of visual outcomes but also aids clinicians in anticipating anatomical changes relevant to visual function.

Fung et al. developed a fully automated DL model to predict the anatomical outcome of RRD surgery using fundus images from the British and Eire Association of Vitreoretinal Surgeons (BEAVRS) database. Their model was based on Inception v3, which is a deep convolutional neural network architecture designed for image classification that uses Inception modules to efficiently achieve high accuracy. An Inception module applies multiple convolutional filters and pooling operations in parallel, then concatenates their outputs to capture both fine details and broader patterns while keeping computation manageable. It was trained on over 6661 images, achieving excellent predictive performance, with an AUC of 0.94, sensitivity of 73.3%, and specificity of 96%. These results demonstrate the potential of DL to assist in preoperative risk stratification and surgical decision-making for patients undergoing RRD repair [9].

In a different study, Zgolli et al. developed an ML-based medical decision support system (MDSS) to predict anatomical outcomes following vitrectomy and inverted-flap internal limiting membrane (ILM) peeling for idiopathic MH. MDSS is a tool that uses ML algorithms to analyze clinical data and provide healthcare professionals with evidence-based insights to support diagnosis, treatment, and patient care decisions. Using preoperative OCT parameters from 120 eyes, the model incorporated multiple MH indices including hole diameter, basal diameter, hole form factor, and area-based metrics. The MDSS achieved excellent performance, with an AUC of 0.984 and a kappa value of 0.934 for predicting MH closure at 9 months postoperatively, suggesting that ML-driven tools may enhance surgical planning and outcome prediction for MH management [23].

Another study evaluated AI-based analysis of OCT biomarkers to predict functional outcomes after ERM surgery using preoperative and postoperative scans from 40 eyes. They employed a semi-supervised DL algorithm (Ophthal) to segment IRF and subretinal fluid (SRF) volumes, count HRF, and calculate percentages of ELM and EZ interruption. A semi-supervised DL algorithm is a model that learns from a combination of labeled and unlabeled data, allowing automated segmentation and quantification of retinal structures while adapting to variations in real-world datasets. Postoperatively, BCVA improved and CMT decreased significantly. IRF volume also decreased significantly, particularly within the central 1-mm subfield, whereas no significant changes were found in SRF, HRF, or EZ/ELM interruptions. Multivariate analysis demonstrated that the preoperative distribution of IRF, especially in the central subfield, correlated independently with BCVA recovery (R2 = 0.38; Pillai’s trace = 0.79). In this study, AI-enabled volumetric analysis of IRF revealed that both the reduction and peripheral redistribution of fluid were strongly associated with improved visual outcomes. Additionally, preoperative EZ and ELM integrity remained critical prognostic indicators, emphasizing the value of precise structural assessment in surgical planning for ERM patients [24].

Furthermore, Xiao et al. developed DL models to assist in the classification and surgical outcome prediction of MH. Using OCT images and clinical data from 330 eyes across four centers, they trained a VGG model to distinguish idiopathic from secondary MH (AUC 0.965, accuracy 95.0%) and a multimodal deep fusion network to predict MH closure 1 month after vitrectomy and ILM peeling (AUC 0.904, accuracy 82.5%). A multimodal deep fusion network is a model that combines information from multiple data modalities (in this case OCT images and clinical data) into a unified representation to improve prediction or decision-making performance. For idiopathic MH specifically, the closure prediction model achieved an AUC of 0.947 and accuracy of 87.5%. The authors validated their DL models on an independent external dataset from a separate ophthalmic center [25].

Another study evaluated ML models for predicting anatomical outcomes after MH surgery using preoperative OCT data from 308 eyes. The random forest model showed the best performance, with 92% accuracy, 94% precision, 97% recall, and an 8.8% misclassification rate. As mentioned previously, a random forest is an ML method that makes predictions by combining the results of many decision trees. Each tree looks at different parts of the data, and their answers are combined (by voting or averaging) to achieve a more accurate and reliable result than a single tree. The MH area (MHA) emerged as the strongest predictor of postoperative success, supporting the role of ML in improving surgical planning for MH patients [26].

Ozturk et al. evaluated the predictive value of OCT-derived indices and AI modeling using ChatGPT-4 to forecast anatomical success following MH surgery. The model, using masked preoperative data, achieved accuracy of 77.0% and an AUC of 0.770, with a perfect PPV (1.000) but low NPV (0.452). A logistic regression model using the same variables demonstrated superior predictive performance (accuracy 84.3%, AUC 0.759, NPV 0.800, κ 0.568). Their findings confirm the prognostic utility of geometric OCT indices and highlight that, although GPT-based AI shows potential in MH outcome prediction, traditional logistic regression currently provides more reliable performance. Future AI models integrating multimodal imaging and clinical data may enhance preoperative surgical planning and individualized prognostication [27].

Lastly, Mitamura et al. applied a DL-based semantic segmentation model to objectively analyze surgical outcomes in vitrectomy with and without cystotomy (direct puncture and aspiration of fluid in cystoid macular edema) for refractory cystoid diabetic macular edema (DME). Thirty-five eyes of 29 patients undergoing vitrectomy were divided into a cystotomy group and an ILM peeling-only group. Using OCT images, areas of macular fluid (MF) were automatically quantified with the Hokkaido University MF segmentation model, a modified deep U-Net incorporating multiple dilated convolutional blocks for improved feature extraction (AUC 0.986, accuracy 0.993, Dice 0.702). The model enabled separate measurement of inner (IMF) and outer (OMF) macular fluid compartments. The cystotomy group demonstrated significantly greater reduction in central retinal thickness at 1 month and a significant decrease in OMF area compared with the ILM peeling group, suggesting that cystotomy may facilitate early postoperative resolution of outer retinal fluid, while DL-assisted OCT analysis provided an objective and reproducible tool for quantifying surgical effects in DME [28].

While these studies show AI’s potential in predicting postoperative anatomical integrity, they are mostly retrospective, lack multicenter validation, and rarely address model interpretability or surgical workflow impact, with synthetic image generation being a promising yet clinically tentative frontier due to regulatory and explainability challenges, necessitating rigorous prospective trials and clinician–AI collaboration.

sec.title: Intraoperative AI Applications
sec.p: While most AI applications in VR surgery focus on preoperative diagnostics or postoperative outcomes, a smaller but growing body of work has explored intraoperative uses, particularly in real-time surgical documentation and decision support. These applications are emerging as essential tools to enhance surgical efficiency, standardization, and data-driven feedback.

Nespolo et al. developed a DL-based platform for real-time detection and segmentation of surgical instruments and key anatomical structures during VR surgery. Using annotated microscope images from 101 patients, an adapted YOLACT++ neural network achieved high segmentation accuracy, with mean area under the precision–recall curve (AUPR) of 0.972 for vitrector tooltips, 0.928 for the optic disc, and 0.916 for macular holes. YOLACT++ (“You Only Look At CoefficienTs”) is a real-time instance segmentation neural network that detects objects in an image and creates a mask for each one. It works by generating a set of prototype masks and combining them with object predictions, making segmentation fast and efficient. The system operated at up to 87 frames per second (FPS), enabling real-time performance. This technology lays the groundwork for AI-assisted surgical guidance in VR procedures, with potential applications in collision avoidance, intraoperative navigation, and surgical data science [29].

Baldi et al. looked into computer vision and developed a DL-based system for real-time 3D tracking of VR surgical tools in a model eye [30]. Using over 100,000 annotated frames, they trained two models: a ResNet-18 classifier and a YOLOv5 detector. ResNet-18 is a type of deep neural network (DNN) designed to recognize patterns in images by passing them through multiple layers; it is especially good at avoiding common training problems by using “shortcuts” between layers. In this system, it was used to identify the type of instrument, which hand it was inserted with, how deep it was, and its coarse x–y position, reaching up to 100% accuracy for instrument type and laterality with inference speeds of 1111 fps. YOLOv5, a fast object detection network, was used to draw boxes around instruments and pinpoint their tips, achieving 79.3% mean average precision (mAP) at 67 FPS. The system generated real-time overlays of tool type, depth, and tip location, showing the potential for AI to guide surgeons, prevent errors, and support training in retinal procedures.

A major limitation in translating AI to the operating room is the absence of real-time imaging integration with AI systems capable of guiding maneuver precision or detecting complications on the fly. Unlike in other specialties such as neurosurgery or robotic urology, where real-time AI-assisted navigation has seen preliminary success, ophthalmology lags behind in this domain.

Nevertheless, the clinical impact of these tools remains modest unless integrated with feedback loops to surgeons, real-time image recognition, or procedural planning systems. Future work should prioritize multimodal models that merge intraoperative imaging with instrument tracking and predictive analytics to advance toward true AI-assisted surgery.

sec.title: Risk Stratification and Complication Prediction
sec.p: AI-driven risk stratification and complication prediction represent crucial tools for improving surgical planning and patient counseling in VR procedures. These models often aim to identify high-risk patients preoperatively by analyzing structured clinical data, imaging, or both.

Antaki et al. demonstrated that ophthalmologists without coding expertise could develop ML models to predict proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR) using AutoML platforms [31]. These are tools that automatically handle the process of building and optimizing ML models, including tasks like data preprocessing, feature selection, and model tuning. They make it easier for users to create effective ML models without the need for deep expertise in ML. This retrospective study of 506 eyes used clinical features extracted from electronic health records and achieved an AUC of 0.90 with a quadratic support vector machine, alongside sensitivity of 63.0% and specificity of 97.8%. This study also emphasized the importance of class balancing and feature selection, especially in low-incidence outcomes like PVR.

In another study, Li et al. developed a cascaded DL system using UWF fundus images to detect RD and assess macular status [10]. This is a model architecture where multiple neural networks are connected in sequence, with each stage refining or building upon the output of the previous one. This approach improves accuracy by breaking complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps handled progressively. Using 11,087 images, their first model distinguished RD from non-RD with an AUC of 0.989, sensitivity of 96.1%, and specificity of 99.6%. A second model identified macula-on RD with an AUC of 0.975, sensitivity of 93.8%, and specificity of 90.9%. Agreement with expert grading was high, with Cohen’s κ of 0.965 for RD and 0.811 for macular status. Beyond diagnostic accuracy, the system also generated heatmap-guided arrows or circles on images to advise patient head positioning preoperatively, an innovation aimed at reducing RD progression between detection and surgical repair. This model was externally validated using a different UWF imaging system at another institution.

Maywood et al. evaluated ChatGPT’s ability to provide accurate and comprehensive responses to complex VR clinical scenarios. In this cross-sectional study, the chatbot (ChatGPT 3.5) answered 83% of 40 open-ended scenarios correctly but delivered comprehensive responses in only 52.5% of cases. Subgroup analysis revealed the highest accuracy for diabetic retinopathy (100%) and lower accuracy for surgical scenarios (70%). Among six incorrect responses, two were judged as potentially harmful to patients. The findings showed ChatGPT’s potential to assist with ophthalmic education but also emphasize limitations in comprehensiveness and the risk of misinformation in clinical contexts [32].

Another study developed DL models to automatically segment SRF from OCT B-scan images in 45 patients with rhegmatogenous retinal detachment, using a total of 3819 slices across 98 time points. The best-performing model was a SegFormer vision transformer, which achieved a median Dice score of 0.92, an average mean Dice of 0.82, and an average total Dice of 0.94 across fivefold cross-validation and hold-out testing. A Dice score is a metric used to measure the similarity of two sets, commonly used in image segmentation tasks to evaluate how well a model’s predicted segmentation matches the ground truth. A SegFormer is a DL model designed for semantic segmentation that splits an image into patches, encodes both local and global contextual features using a transformer-based architecture, and outputs pixel-wise segmentation masks. This model was pretrained on large public OCT datasets and then fine-tuned on an in-house dataset to improve its accuracy and generalizability. The SegFormer model demonstrated robust performance and consistency [33].

Pereira et al. developed an automated AI model to assess MH volume on swept-source OCT images. In this proof-of-concept case series of 24 patients with idiopathic FTMH, a fully three-dimensional convolutional neural network (3D CNN) was trained using manually segmented MH volumes. This is a neural network that applies 3D convolutional filters to process volumetric data, such as medical scans or video sequences. By analyzing depth, height, and width simultaneously, it can capture spatial and temporal features more effectively than 2D CNNs. The automated model demonstrated a strong correlation with manual measurements (R2 = 0.94) and outperformed the conventional minimum linear diameter in predicting postoperative VA at 1 year (volume: R2 = 0.53; diameter: R2 = 0.39). These findings highlight the potential of AI-based volumetric analysis for improved MH assessment and outcome prediction [34].

Another study developed DL models to predict late recurrence of RRD using pre- and postoperative UWF pseudocolor and autofluorescence (AF) fundus images. In this retrospective study of 412 eyes with at least 2 years of follow-up after primary or recurrent RRD repair, the models achieved high predictive performance, with postoperative pseudocolor UWF images yielding up to 91.1% accuracy in scleral buckle (SB) cases and 90.2% in pars plana vitrectomy cases. AF-based models similarly achieved over 91% accuracy postoperatively. The study demonstrates the potential of DL for stratifying recurrence risk following RRD repair and highlights novel imaging-derived risk factors such as extensive laser retinopexy and asymmetric staphyloma. The authors performed external validation on an independent cohort from a separate institution. This validation is essential in confirming generalizability across centers, imaging conditions, and surgical practices [35].

Despite strong performance metrics, these studies share common limitations. Most are based on retrospective data, often from single centers, raising questions about model generalizability. Additionally, many omit uncertainty quantification, which is critical when managing high-stakes complications. Moreover, there remains a significant gap in regulatory frameworks, explainability, and integration within clinical decision support systems.

To be clinically actionable, risk prediction tools must be embedded in broader care pathways and tested in prospective, multicenter studies. Additionally, models need to incorporate longitudinal patient data to better account for time-dependent risk factors and surgical decision-making dynamics.

sec.title: Patient Support and Education
sec.p: The use of AI to support patients through education, expectation management, and decision-making in VR surgery is a growing area of interest. Our group explored early on the application of large language models (LLMs) in the context of VR diseases [36]. We evaluated the ability of three LLMs—ChatGPT-3.5 (OpenAI), Bing AI (powered by GPT-4 via Microsoft), and Docs-GPT Beta (OpenAI’s medically optimized model)—to answer clinical questions relevant to both patients and providers. While the aim of the paper was not to determine the best LLM, all models demonstrated acceptable and competent performance in answering common questions from patients, with ChatGPT-3.5 offering the most detailed information.

Building upon that foundation, Momenaei et al. assessed the appropriateness and readability of ChatGPT-4-generated responses regarding surgical management of retinal diseases, including RD, MH, and ERM [37]. Two retina specialists graded responses to standardized queries and found consistent appropriateness in 84.6% of RD questions, 92.0% for MH, and 91.7% for ERM; at least one inappropriate answer occurred in 5.1%, 8.0%, and 8.3% of questions, respectively. Readability analysis revealed that responses required a mean Flesch–Kincaid grade level of approximately 14, with reading ease scores between 32 and 34, suggesting college-level comprehension. These findings highlight the potential of LLMs as a supplementary resource in ophthalmic care, while also emphasizing the need for oversight and readability improvements before clinical deployment. However, readability remained low for both models, requiring a college-level education to understand the responses. They also conducted a comparative analysis of ChatGPT-3.5 and ChatGPT-4 in generating responses to standardized questions about surgical treatment for VR pathologies [38]. ChatGPT-4 outperformed ChatGPT-3.5, with appropriate responses in 93.2% of questions, versus only 79.6%. However, readability remained a concern, with the Flesch reading ease scores being significantly lower in ChatGPT-4 than in ChatGPT-3.5. These findings underscore ChatGPT-4’s improved clinical accuracy over its predecessor, while also highlighting the ongoing need for simplification and expert oversight before patient-facing use. The authors emphasize the need for further improvements in AI chatbot performance, particularly regarding clarity and accessibility of patient-facing medical information.

Despite these advances, limitations persist. Most tools lack validation in non-English-speaking populations or in patients with low health literacy. Moreover, ethical issues around misinformation, bias, and privacy remain unresolved. Patient-facing AI must therefore be co-developed with diverse patient populations and subject to regulatory oversight to ensure safe, inclusive, and equitable implementation.

sec.title: Workflow Optimization and Surgical Planning
sec.p: Beyond clinical outcome prediction, artificial intelligence is increasingly being explored as a tool to enhance workflow efficiency and surgical planning in VR surgery. Recent studies have highlighted the potential of AI to support decision-making, automate documentation, and optimize resource management. From AI chatbots assisting with surgical planning to ML models improving procedural coding accuracy, these applications illustrate how AI can streamline administrative processes and reduce human error, ultimately contributing to more efficient and precise surgical care.

A study by Carlà et al. assessed the ability of three LLMs—ChatGPT-3.5, ChatGPT-4, and Google Gemini—to suggest surgical planning for RD cases. In 50 RD cases, these LLMs agreed with expert VR surgeons in 80%, 84%, and 70% of cases, respectively. ChatGPT-4 achieved the highest Global Quality Score (GQS) (4.2 ± 0.7), outperforming both ChatGPT-3.5 (3.9 ± 0.8) and Gemini (3.5 ± 1.1). Errors were mainly related to endotamponade selection, with ChatGPT-4 being the only model to suggest combined phacovitrectomy. These results highlight the potential of AI chatbots to assist in surgical planning for RD management, though further refinement is needed [39].

Lee et al. explored in two papers the capabilities of ML in dealing with operation notes in VR surgery. They developed ML and natural language processing (NLP) models to improve the accuracy of procedural coding in ophthalmology, with a particular focus on vitrectomy. NLPs are a type of AI that understand, interpret, and generate human language from text or speech. They can perform tasks like translation, summarization, and question-answering by learning patterns in language data. In a multicenter study of 1000 operation notes, their best-performing model Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) achieved 88.0% classification accuracy across the five most common procedures, demonstrating the potential to reduce administrative burden and increase reimbursement accuracy. A separate study focusing on 617 vitrectomy operation notes revealed that 66.5% of procedural codes were initially missed, amounting to nearly half of potential reimbursement. Their XGBoost model achieved 94.6% accuracy in detecting missing codes, with an AUC of 0.87 for identifying operation notes with two or more omissions. These results support the use of combined human and AI-led approaches to procedural coding, offering a pathway to improve healthcare documentation, reimbursement, and resource planning in ophthalmic surgery [40, 41].

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our review has revealed how novel prognostic models, especially those combining multimodal imaging with clinical data, have shown encouraging results in predicting both visual and anatomical outcomes clinically in VR. AI is no longer confined to experimental prototypes in VR surgery, but it is also beginning to show some practical applications in everyday clinical practice. Computer vision is proving to potentially allow real-time support in the operating room for RRD, ERM or MH surgery. Risk-stratification tools may enable us to distinguish which cases might develop complications, and at the same time, LLM-based systems are already useful in automating notes, coding, and even patient counseling. Together, these advances suggest that AI is about effectively supporting VR surgeons’ focus on clinically meaningful decisions, rather than replacing surgeons’ judgment entirely [42].
For postoperative BCVA prediction, most models focused on OCT-based analysis, frequently combined with clinical parameters to improve prognostic accuracy.

Simple models based on ML algorithms showed how AI-derived OCT biomarkers representing the specific retinal layers may serve as reliable prognostic factors for predicting BCVA after surgery [11, 12]. Going down this path, several studies have explored more complex DL architectures, which generally outperformed traditional ML approaches. For example, Wen et al.’s multimodal deep fusion network and Yeh et al.’s heterogeneous data fusion net achieved R2 values up to 0.80 and AUCs near 98%, respectively [6, 15].

Beyond OCT, multimodal approaches incorporating fundus imaging and clinical data, such as the model by Guo et al. for RRD surgery, have also shown promising results (AUC 0.91, accuracy 86%) [20]. These findings suggest that the integration of structural OCT biomarkers with broader retinal multimodal imaging and patient-level variables can further improve the final predictive robustness; however, some limitations are still present at the current state of the art, with the majority of these studies being retrospective, single-center analyses with low demographic diversity, raising concerns about external generalizability. In fact, an important challenge for upcoming AI-based studies in VR surgery will be addressing bias, particularly that related to ethnic and socioeconomic disparities, which remain evident in current predictive models. To promote equitable AI deployment, future studies should intentionally curate datasets that reflect broad ethnic, geographic, and socioeconomic diversity, rather than relying on convenience samples from homogeneous populations. Explicit reporting of demographic composition, along with subgroup performance analyses and bias-mitigation strategies during model training, will be essential to ensure that AI tools perform reliably across the full spectrum of patients encountered in VR practice [42].

Similarly, anatomical outcome prediction studies, particularly for MH closure and RRD reattachment, report high performance metrics, such as the AUC of 0.984 for Zgolli et al.’s MH closure model and the 92% accuracy of Venkatesh et al.’s random forest approach [23, 26]. Generative models capable of synthesizing postoperative OCT scans, such as those explored by Kwon et al. and Kim et al., have proven to be capable of offering an exciting opportunity for patient education and expectation management [7, 8]. The clinical adoption of these algorithms will depend on showing that these tools can genuinely inform choices, improve communication, and translate into better clinical outcomes, rather than remaining well-performing but isolated academic exercises.

AI applications are now extending beyond outcome prediction into intraoperative and administrative domains. For example, Nespolo and Baldi demonstrated the feasibility of real-time instrument tracking and anatomical structure segmentation during VR surgery, laying the foundation for future AI-assisted navigation [29, 30]. On the administrative side, Lee et al. developed models for procedural coding of vitrectomy cases that showed strong potential to reduce missed codes and optimize reimbursement, tackling long-standing inefficiencies in clinical documentation. Similarly, Carla et al.’s evaluation of LLMs such as ChatGPT-4 and Google Gemini in RD surgical planning suggests that AI chatbots could play a future role in clinical decision support, though their current performance still falls short of expert standards in complex surgical scenarios [39]. Ultimately, while these intraoperative and administrative applications are promising proofs of concept, their current impact on surgical practice is still limited, and without rigorous validation they risk remaining technological curiosities rather than tools that meaningfully improve patient care.

Despite progress, translating AI into routine VR surgery still faces several difficulties. First, the evidence base remains relatively thin: most models are trained retrospectively on single-center datasets, limiting both generalizability and equity [42]. Without prospective, multicenter validation, these tools risk looking stronger on paper than they will in real-world practice. Second, interpretability is still a bottleneck. Many DL approaches behave as “black boxes,” which may reduce clinician confidence and complicate regulatory approval [43, 44]. Building uncertainty-aware and transparent models is essential if predictions are to responsibly guide surgical decisions. Third, the scope of inputs remains narrow. Current models are often confined to OCT or fundus imaging, each capturing only part of the disease process. The next step should focus on integrating multimodal imaging with clinical and surgical data, and eventually leveraging foundation models in order to provide a more holistic and reliable view of prognosis [45].

Beyond these technical issues, there is a human dimension that should not be overlooked. Prediction is not just about numbers; it is about how results are communicated, how expectations are managed, and how trust is built between patient and surgeon. AI will only add value if it improves these conversations and decisions at the bedside.

Across all application domains, external validation emerges as a critical determinant of clinical readiness. While several studies report excellent performance metrics, only a minority have demonstrated consistent results on independent datasets acquired from different institutions, imaging platforms, or patient populations. External validation is essential for distinguishing models that genuinely generalize from those that are overfitted to local practice patterns or imaging protocols. Without robust multicenter external validation, even highly accurate AI systems risk underperforming when deployed in real-world VR clinics, limiting their safety, trustworthiness, and translational impact. The use of external datasets for validation needs to be increased for future studies if we want to achieve low risk of bias for vitreoretinal AI research.

In addition to external validation, in the near future, progress will depend on collaboration among VR specialists, data scientists, and regulators, with a shared focus on transparency, diversity of datasets, and clinically meaningful endpoints. Translating AI models from retrospective, external validation research into clinical deployment will require a structured development pathway, moving from technical validation to prospective, multicenter clinical evaluation and real-world performance monitoring. Early alignment with regulatory frameworks, including Food and Drug Administration (FDA) clearance or CE marking for Software as a Medical Device, is essential to define intended use, acceptable risk, and evidence requirements. Addressing these considerations during model development will help ensure that promising AI tools can progress from experimental studies to safe and effective integration into routine VR care.

This review has several limitations. The studies that were included are highly heterogeneous in terms of AI methodology, clinical tasks, datasets, outcome metrics, and validation strategies, precluding quantitative synthesis and limiting direct comparability. Additionally, the majority of the evidence is retrospective and proof-of-concept in nature, with relatively few studies demonstrating true external or multicenter validation. This constrains conclusions about generalizability and clinical readiness. Furthermore, although a structured assessment of risk of bias was incorporated, this evaluation relied on the completeness and transparency of reporting in the original studies, which varied. Finally, given the rapidly evolving nature of AI technologies, particularly LLMs and multimodal systems, some findings may quickly become outdated as new methods, datasets, and regulatory standards emerge.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: AI is beginning to shape vitreoretinal surgery, with its application ranging from postoperative outcome prediction to intraoperative guidance and workflow optimization. Current models demonstrate strong performance in controlled settings, but most remain limited by retrospective design, narrow datasets, and a lack of external validation. The critical next step will be rigorous, prospective evaluation to determine whether these tools can reliably augment surgical decision-making and improve patient outcomes in real-world practice.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p:
Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.

notes.title: Author Contributions
notes.p: Conceptualization: Enrico Bernardi, Rodrigo Anguita. Methodology: Enrico Bernardi, Rodrigo Anguita. Validation: Lorenzo Ferro Desideri, Neil Shah, Carla Troyas, Yousif Subhi, Ben Kirkpatrick, Martin Zinkernagel. Writing–Review & Editing: Enrico Bernardi, Rodrigo Anguita, Lorenzo Ferro Desideri, Neil Shah, Carla Troyas, Yousif Subhi, Ben Kirkpatrick, Martin Zinkernagel. Supervision: Rodrigo Anguita.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: No funding or sponsorship was received for this study or publication of this article.

notes.title: Data Availability
notes.p: All data generated or analyzed during this study are either included in this published article/as supplementary information files, or publicly available.

notes.title: Conflict of interest
notes.p: Enrico Bernardi, Lorenzo Ferro Desideri, Neil Shah, Carla Troyas, Ben Kirkpatrick and Martin Zinkernagel have nothing to disclose. Yousif Subhi is a Section Editor of Ophthalmology and Therapy. Yousif Subhi was not involved in the selection of peer reviewers for the manuscript nor any of the subsequent editorial decisions. Rodrigo Anguita is an Editorial Board member of Ophthalmology and Therapy. Rodrigo Anguita was not involved in the selection of peer reviewers for the manuscript nor any of the subsequent editorial decisions. All other authors have nothing to disclose.

notes.title: Ethical Approval
notes.p: This article is based on previously conducted studies and does not contain any new studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
